data.title: Altria declares $1.02 dividend

  • See MO Dividend Scorecard, Yield Chart, & Dividend Growth.

data.title: North American Construction Group targets $4B backlog midyear as infrastructure and Australia drive expansion
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: North American Construction Group Ltd. (NOA) Q1 2025

Management View

  • Joe Lambert, President and CEO, reported a record trailing 12-month combined revenue of $1.5 billion, highlighting, "We expanded our heavy equipment fleet in Australia by over 10%, boosting capacity to meet growing demand." He noted a 68% equipment utilization rate in Canada and Australia, with Canadian fleet achieving its best quarterly utilization since winter 2022-2023 and February peaking at 70%. Early-stage work at a major copper mine in New South Wales was cited as positioning the company for long-term value in critical minerals. The Fargo project moved past 65% completion, with final construction underway in Q2. Lambert emphasized, "Our parts and component supply and services agreement with Finning delivered a full quarter of impact, effectively combining our in-house capabilities with their expertise to drive improving cost and equipment utilization."
  • Jason Veenstra, CFO, stated, "The headline EBITDA number of $100 million and the correlated 25.5% margin were both negatively impacted by the weather in Australia and Canada." He added, "The $392 million of revenue this quarter represents an overall increase of 18%." Veenstra also disclosed that Canadian operations posted $178 million in top-line revenue, 45% higher than Q2 2024, and that Australian operations saw a $24 million increase despite weather disruptions.
  • Lambert highlighted the company’s unchanged 2025 priorities: safety, equipment utilization, geographic and commodity diversification, customer satisfaction, and expansion of internal maintenance skills. He also announced, "We have a new executive member starting with us in a couple of months, and she will be leading our Infrastructure business in what we see as an exciting area for growth."

Outlook

  • Management’s 2025 outlook remains unchanged, with expectations to recover from Q1 weather impacts and a busy December construction season in North America. Lambert stated, "We believe we can make up for the Q1 weather impacts in both Australia and Canada over the course of the year and expect December construction activity in North America will be busy, particularly in Q3."
  • The company projects its backlog to reach a record $4 billion by midyear, supported by a strong bid pipeline of $15 billion and increasing opportunities in infrastructure and renewals in Australia.

Financial Results

  • Combined revenue for the quarter was $392 million. Veenstra reported EBITDA of $100 million with a margin of 25.5%. Canadian operations delivered $178 million in revenue, and Australian operations saw $24 million growth despite weather. Gross profit margin was reported at 13.2%, with weather estimated to have impacted margins by 5% to 7%. Adjusted earnings per share were $0.52, reflecting steady EBITDA but "significantly impacted by the $11 million of increased depreciation," as per Veenstra.
  • Net cash provided by operations prior to working capital was $76 million. Free cash flow usage was impacted by front-loaded capital maintenance and a $25 million working capital draw. Net debt ended at $867 million, with subsequent issuance of $225 million in senior unsecured notes. Return on invested capital was 10.6% at quarter-end, with normalization expected to trend back to 15% as new assets and projects contribute fully.

Q&A

  • Adam Thalhimer, Thompson Davis, asked about seasonality and Q2 trends. Veenstra responded, "We actually see top line and EBITDA being quite consistent with Q1... with lower depreciation, we see on the EPS side, a nice increase in Q2."
  • Thalhimer inquired about infrastructure bidding. Lambert explained, "There's a couple of dozen actually. It's all around energy transition and climate resiliency... Most of the stuff you'll see on our bid chart is actually from the P3 conference in Dallas in the U.S. and that's really what's driving that part of the business."
  • John Gibson, BMO Capital Markets, questioned the financial impact of the rainy weather in Australia. Veenstra clarified, "We put it at about 5% to 7% of gross profit margin in Australia," and detailed a drop in margins from a normal 25% to 16-17%.
  • Chris Thompson, CIBC, asked about quarterly EBITDA cadence and guidance. Veenstra reiterated, "We do see Q2 looking a lot like Q1 on the EBITDA perspective... about 55% being in the second half of the year with 45% in the first half."
  • Devin Schilling, Ventum Financial, inquired about contract renewals and U.S. infrastructure pursuits. Lambert stated, "We've had 100% renewal rate," and regarding a California bid, "We were unsuccessful in that... but from what we've added... we've added 3, 4 or 5 that you'll see on the bid pipeline."

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts maintained a neutral to slightly positive tone, repeatedly recognizing management’s performance amid challenging weather. Questions centered on seasonality, weather impacts, margin sustainability, and backlog growth, without overt skepticism.
  • Management conveyed cautious confidence, acknowledging weather-driven setbacks but expressing certainty about recovery and growth. Lambert stressed, "Our job is to deliver results that we say we are going to get... and that's our expectation."
  • Compared to the previous quarter, sentiment from both analysts and management held steady, with analysts focusing on operational resilience and management reiterating commitment to guidance and execution.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • Q1 saw continued weather-related challenges in both Canada and Australia, affecting margins and utilization, whereas Q4 benefited from more stable operating conditions and higher margins.
  • Guidance language remained consistent, with management reaffirming recovery plans for weather setbacks and projecting significant backlog growth midyear, compared to a record-setting backlog at Q4 close.
  • Analysts’ focus shifted toward the pace of recovery from weather issues, contract renewals, and infrastructure expansion. Management’s tone remained confident but more focused on contingency planning for disruptive weather.
  • Key metric changes included a drop in gross profit margin from Q4’s 14.6% (adjusted) to 13.2%, and increased depreciation expense impacting adjusted EPS.
  • Strategic focus on Australian and infrastructure growth strengthened, with an explicit target to build infrastructure to 25% of the business over the next three years.

Risks and Concerns

  • Management cited weather volatility as a primary risk, estimating a 5% to 7% margin impact from Q1 conditions. Lambert also addressed the potential impact of U.S. tariffs, stating current exposure is "negligible."
  • Analyst concerns centered on the sustainability of contract renewals, margin normalization, and ability to meet guidance amid persistent climate-driven disruptions.
  • Management outlined mitigation through operational efficiency, diversified geographic footprint, and cost discipline.

Final Takeaway

North American Construction Group management emphasized that despite a weather-impacted start to 2025, the company remains on track to deliver its unchanged full-year guidance, driven by robust Australian expansion, a growing infrastructure pipeline, and a record $4 billion backlog target by midyear. Leadership underscored confidence in operational resilience, ongoing contract renewals, and the ability to capitalize on long-term civil infrastructure and resource opportunities in key markets.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: Arm, AMD in focus as Q1 CPU data shows share gains for pair: Evercore
data.description:
Arm Holdings (NASDAQ:ARM) and AMD (NASDAQ:AMD) were in focus on Thursday as recent industry data showed the pair increased their CPU market share in the first-quarter, Evercore ISI said.

“We view the market share report and recent news items particularly favorable for AMD and ARM  –  particularly on share gains for ARM in Server and Notebooks, and server share gains and client [average selling price] increases for AMD,” Evercore analyst Mark Lipacis wrote in a note to clients, referencing data from Mercury Research.

Lipacis reiterated his Outperform rating on both Arm and AMD.

The Mercury Research data showed that Arm increased its unit share in servers by 400 basis points year-over-year to 10%, while AMD increased its share by 230 basis points to 24%. Conversely, Intel (INTC) lost share in servers during the quarter, ending the period with 65% of the market, down 630 basis points year-over-year and 210 basis points from the fourth-quarter of 2024.

Looking ahead, Lipacis said it'ss likely that Arm will continue to take gains in servers, as company management said on its most recent earnings call that it expects “up to 50% of new server chips at hyperscalers to be Arm based this year.” And with Nvidia (NVDA), Amazon (AMZN) and Google (GOOG) (GOOGL) seeing strong adoption for their Grace, Graviton and Axion offerings respectively, Arm should reap the rewards, Lipacis posited.

Additionally, Microsoft's (MSFT) offerings are seeing “superior performance per watt” for workloads like Microsoft Teams, Azure SQL and more. And some of Microsoft's customers, such as Snowflake (SNOW), Templafy and others are using Microsoft's Cobalt Azure virtual machines for some of their workloads.

Specific to AMD, the average selling price of its offerings have outperformed those of Intel, having declined just 2% year-over-year, compared to an 11% drop for Intel.

“AMD’s [average selling prices] appear more resilient due to Turin ramp, while INTC’s saw [year-over-year] headwinds from higher hyperscale demand and higher demand for older products / edge processing units,” Lipacis wrote in a note to clients.

data.title: FDA to issue new vaccine framework within weeks
data.description:
FDA Commissioner Marty Makary said Thursday that his agency is preparing to release a new regulatory framework within the next few weeks clarifying its guidance related to vaccines, particularly COVID-19 shots.

“We want to be very transparent, and we want to create a framework for vaccine makers that they can use so they have a predictable FDA where they don’t have to worry,” Makary said in a fireside chat at the annual conference of the Food and Drug Law Institute.

He added that Vinay Prasad, the recently appointed director of the FDA’s Center for Biologics Evaluation and Research (CBER), is in discussions with the industry and agency staffers as he looks to unveil a “massive framework,” incorporating the agency’s regulatory stance on vaccines.

His remarks come at a time when industry is looking for clarity regarding the agency’s expectations for vaccines following the confirmation of Robert F. Kennedy Jr., a well-known vaccine critic, as the secretary of the Health and Human Services Department, which oversees the FDA.

COVID-19 vaccine makers: Pfizer (NYSE:PFE)/ BioNTech (NASDAQ:BNTX), Moderna (NASDAQ:MRNA), Novavax (NASDAQ:NVAX)

Leading vaccine makers: GSK (GSK), Sanofi (SNY), Merck (MRK), AstraZeneca (AZN).

data.title: UnitedHealth Group's sharp decline sends shockwaves through the ETF market
data.description:
The continued plunge in shares of UnitedHealth Group (NYSE:UNH) is sending ripples far beyond individual investors. As the health care giant faces intensifying scrutiny, market participants holding certain exchange traded funds are also feeling the pressure. UNH’s persistent downturn is not just a company-specific event—it’s reverberating throughout the broader financial landscape.

Currently, UnitedHealth is held by 391 ETFs, with more than 142 million shares owned across these investment vehicles. This extensive exposure has made the stock's steep decline a significant drag on a wide swath of funds, affecting a range of institutional and retail investors alike. 

The drop in UNH's stock comes amid growing legal troubles. The company is now under investigation by the U.S. Department of Justice for potential Medicare fraud, casting a shadow over its operations and stoking investor concerns. 

On Thursday UNH trades lower by 13.1% and bigger picture the company is down by 47.8% in 2025. 

Listed below are Wall Street’s 10 exchange traded funds that have the largest portfolio allocations towards UNH: 

No. 10: Global X Dow 30 Covered Call & Growth ETF (NYSEARCA:DYLG), 4.66% allocation.

No. 9: Global X Dow 30 Covered Call ETF (NYSEARCA:DJIA), 4.81% allocation.

No. 8: ProShares Ultra Health Care (NYSEARCA:RXL), 4.81% allocation.

No. 7: Future Health Care Equity ETF (NYSEARCA:GDOC), 4.85% allocation.

No. 6: Fidelity MSCI Health Care Index ETF (NYSEARCA:FHLC), 5.18% allocation.

No. 5: JPMorgan Healthcare Leaders ETF (NASDAQ:JDOC), 5.25% allocation.

No. 4: iShares U.S. Healthcare ETF (NYSEARCA:IYH), 5.87% allocation.

No. 3: Health Care Select Sector SPDR Fund (NYSEARCA:XLV), 5.97% allocation.

No. 2: Vanguard Health Care ETF (NYSEARCA:VHT), 7.92% allocation.

No. 1: iShares U.S. Healthcare Providers ETF (NYSEARCA:IHF), 15.46% allocation.

data.title: BioNTech says bispecific antibody BNT324 is competitive against Summit's ivonescimab
data.description:
BNT324, a bispecific antibody candidate under development by BioNTech (NASDAQ:BNTX) that is seen as critical to the biotech's future success, will compete favorably against rival Summit Therapeutics' (NASDAQ:SMMT) ivonescimab, according to a BioNTech exec.

Speaking at the FT Live US Pharma and Biotech Summit in New York City on Thursday, BioNTech Chief Commercial Officer Annemarie Hanekamp said that the company is planning significant data readouts related to BNT324 this year.

She added that BioNTech operates with a differentiated strategy, as its pipeline includes other types of oncology treatments, such as antibody-drug conjugates as well as other bispecific antibodies, that could potentially be used in combination.

BNT324, which the Germany-based biotech got through its $950M acquisition of Chinese biotech Biotheus in November 2024, combines PD-L1 checkpoint inhibition with VEGF-A neutralization. It is in advanced development for non-small and small cell lung cancer, as well as triple-negative breast cancer. 

Ivonescimab, also a bispecific antibody, is in late-stage development for the same indications, as well as head and neck cancer, biliary tract cancer, and pancreatic cancer.

Summit's asset drew headlines in September 2024 after the stock surged following phase 3 results showing that ivonescimab bested Merck's Keytruda (pembrolizumab) in lung cancer patients.

data.title: MiNK Therapeutics outlines near-term partnering proposals and reduced cash burn as company advances iNKT programs
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: MiNK Therapeutics (INKT) Q1 2025

Management View

  • Jennifer Buell, President and CEO, highlighted progress in three critical areas: clinical advancement, capital efficiency, and strategic partnerships. “This quarter, we've made meaningful progress towards our mission and that's delivering scalable, durable, off-the-shelf iNKT cell therapies to patients with solid tumors and other immune-related diseases.”
  • Buell reported “a further reduction of about 47% year-on-year” in operating cash burn, emphasizing the company's efforts to preserve resources while continuing clinical advancement.
  • Three distinct partnering proposals are under confidential discussion, each aligned with a key therapeutic area—oncology, immunology/inflammatory conditions, and next-generation pipeline assets. Buell stated, “We are advancing confidential discussions for proposals, each of which could extend our runway and accelerate our impact.”
  • On product updates, Buell emphasized new data in solid tumors, especially second-line gastric cancer, showing immune activation and early clinical activity in refractory patients. She also referenced a forthcoming publication on a patient with metastatic testicular cancer who achieved a durable complete response after a single infusion of agenT-797, with “disease stabilization” over 12 months and “complete remission” at 24 months post-treatment.
  • Additional programs in ARDS and GvHD are advancing, with external financing supporting key trials. Buell noted, “We expect the formal award [from NIAID] by June. This would provide critical nondilutive funding.”
  • Christine Klaskin, Principal Financial and Accounting Officer, stated, “We ended the quarter with a cash balance of $3.2 million. Cash used in operations for the three months ended March 31, 2025, was $1.3 million. This is reduced from $2.5 million for the same period in 2024. Our net loss for the first quarter of 2025 was $2.8 million or $0.70 per share.”

Outlook

  • Buell indicated plans to share “additional updated clinical updates later this year and the beginning of next year” for solid tumor programs, and expects a peer-reviewed publication on the testicular cancer case imminently.
  • For the Phase 2 gastric cancer trial, Buell confirmed, “We're still targeting to get something out by the end of this year,” with the latest potential update early next year.
  • The GvHD program is expected to initiate a Phase 1 trial, supported primarily through external partnerships and anticipated NIAID funding, with formal award notification expected in June.

Financial Results

  • Klaskin reported a cash balance of $3.2 million and an operating cash burn of $1.3 million for Q1 2025, down from $2.5 million in Q1 2024. Net loss for Q1 2025 was $2.8 million or $0.70 per share, compared to $3.8 million or $1.10 per share in Q1 2024.
  • Management emphasized continued focus on financial discipline, with reductions in operating burn and internalization of program operations to improve efficiency.

Q&A

  • Emily Bodnar, H.C. Wainwright: Asked about timing and durability of the complete response in testicular cancer, as well as plans for testicular and other indications. Buell replied the complete response was designated at month 24 after a single 797 infusion, with disease reduction in all lesions and highlighted ongoing review of other patient data for broader clinical benefit.
  • Bodnar asked if initial efficacy data from the Phase 2 gastric trial is still on track for the second half. Buell confirmed it is, with updates expected by year-end or early next year.
  • Bodnar inquired about NIAID funding timing amid government delays. Buell stated, “We were reassured to get a formal notification from NIAID that we can expect to hear that we had probable funding and can expect to hear conclusively in June.”
  • Matt Phipps, William Blair: Asked for details on the GvHD trial patient populations and prior treatments. Buell and Thiago Favano explained two Phase 1 programs are ready—one for steroid-refractory acute GvHD, the other for prophylaxis. Both will be discussed with regulators, and funding could support both studies. Favano noted expectations to observe effects on infection prevention and engraftment.

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts focused on timing and specifics of clinical milestones, showing a neutral to slightly positive tone, with appreciation for the durability of responses and funding clarity. Questions were probing but not adversarial.
  • Management maintained a confident and optimistic stance, emphasizing operational discipline, durability of responses, and partnership momentum. Buell’s responses were detailed and transparent, using phrases such as “we're really quite enthusiastic about this” and “we're optimistic.”
  • Compared to the previous quarter, the tone from both management and analysts remains steady, with continued interest in strategic progress and funding, and management projecting assurance about near-term developments and efficiency gains.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • In contrast to last quarter’s focus on leadership changes and the Autonomous Therapeutics partnership, this quarter emphasized partnering proposals and external funding opportunities.
  • While both quarters highlighted clinical data in gastric cancer and ARDS, the current quarter introduced new long-term case detail in testicular cancer and underscored the durability of single-infusion responses.
  • Management’s tone continues to be optimistic but is more focused on imminent partnership and funding decisions, while analysts’ questions remain concentrated on trial timelines, funding status, and clinical durability.
  • Financial discipline and reductions in operating cash burn were highlighted in both quarters, but this quarter provided a more specific year-on-year improvement.

Risks and Concerns

  • Management identified delays in government funding but indicated recent reassurance from NIAID about imminent award decisions.
  • Advancement of the GvHD program remains contingent on external financing, though backup partnerships are being discussed in parallel.
  • Buell noted, “The delays that we have seen globally have impacted us,” but emphasized optimism due to the program’s priority for government agencies.

Final Takeaway

MiNK Therapeutics reported progress in clinical programs and highlighted imminent partnering proposals intended to extend runway and support its iNKT cell therapy platform. The company continues to prioritize financial discipline, with significant reductions in cash burn, while preparing for key clinical data releases in solid tumors and the initiation of new trials in GvHD and ARDS. Management remains optimistic about securing nondilutive funding and advancing external partnerships to accelerate development across multiple therapeutic areas.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: Deere widens 2025 net income outlook to $4.75B–$5.5B amid tariff headwinds and robust tech investment
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Deere & Company (DE) Q2 2025

Management View

  • John May, Chairman and CEO, emphasized the company's focus on resilience amid “historic levels of volatility and significant uncertainty across our end markets, given the dynamic global trade backdrop.” He reiterated the unwavering commitment to customers, stating, “Everything that we do at John Deere starts with our customers who provide food, fuel, clothing, shelter, and infrastructure for the world. We're honored to have served them for nearly 200 years, and we will continue to do so for the next 200.”
  • May confirmed continued investment in the “smart industrial strategy,” highlighting a planned $20 billion investment in the U.S. over the next decade for product development, technology, and manufacturing.
  • CFO Josh Jepsen reported, “John Deere delivered a better than expected second quarter with an 18.8% margin for the equipment operations, demonstrating exceptional execution amidst challenging market dynamics.” Jepsen noted margins exceeded projections due to favorable production costs and efficiency gains, despite tariff headwinds.
  • Jepsen disclosed, “as we look to the second half of the year, global uncertainty continues to weigh on customer sentiment across end markets,” with the company broadening its 2025 outlook range due to a “fluid tariff environment.”
  • Jepsen also commended teams for “exceptional job to position us well as we navigate the current environment,” noting specific mitigation efforts like certification for USMCA exemptions and optimizing global trade flows.

Outlook

  • The company widened its fiscal 2025 net income guidance to a range of $4.75 billion to $5.5 billion, citing tariff and trade policy uncertainty as key variables.
  • May indicated continued capital and R&D investment in integrated solutions, with robust U.S. manufacturing commitments.
  • Guidance for equipment operations margins, production and precision Ag, and construction and forestry segments was adjusted primarily due to expected tariff impacts and shifting market demand.
  • Management stated, “our outlook for net income has widened to between $4.75 billion and $5.5 billion,” maintaining the effective tax rate between 20% and 22% and equipment operations cash flow projection of $4.5 billion to $5.5 billion.

Financial Results

  • Net sales and revenues for the quarter were $12.763 billion, with net sales for equipment operations at $11.171 billion.
  • Net income attributable to Deere & Company was $1.804 billion, or $6.64 per diluted share.
  • Production and precision Ag net sales were $5.23 billion, down 21% year-over-year; operating profit was $1.148 billion with a 22% margin.
  • Small Ag and Turf net sales totaled $2.994 billion, down 6% year-over-year; operating profit was $574 million at a 19.2% margin.
  • Construction and forestry net sales were $2.947 billion, a 23% year-over-year decline; operating profit was $379 million at a 12.9% margin.
  • Financial services net income was $161 million for the quarter, with the full-year forecast unchanged at $750 million.
  • The company reported “roughly $100 million in incremental tariff headwinds” in Q2, forecasting a pre-tax tariff impact of just over $500 million for fiscal year 2025.

Q&A

  • Rob Wertheimer, Melius Research, asked about SaaS models and the pipeline for precision tech. Josh Rohleder explained, “as you think about our SaaS offerings, it's fair to put them in really 3 buckets...precision digital technologies...usage-based technologies...and forthcoming autonomous solutions as a SaaS model as well.” Jepsen added, “I think increasingly, as we progress and as we drive outcomes and utilization, I think you'll see some of those come together more in bundles.”
  • Tim Thein, Raymond James, inquired about implied profitability in the second half for PPA. Jepsen responded, “there's a few things at play...certainly, the tariff impact...you also have a mix impact there, North American large Ag being down...while price is favorable, it's favorable to a less extent than a year ago.”
  • Jamie Cook, Truist Securities, questioned the approach to early order program pricing amid tariffs. Jepsen indicated, “the structure of the early order programs will be similar to the past...we do have structured some optionality in those programs, optionality to wait as we think about the price and discount structure for upcoming phases as tariffs evolve.”
  • David Raso, Evercore ISI, sought clarity on margin softness in the second half. Jepsen detailed, “the impact of tariffs...is about $400 million the rest of the year...being so back-end loaded in the back half margins, that's 2 to 2.5 points in the back half.”
  • Jerry Revich, Goldman Sachs, asked about price cost philosophy in 2026. Jepsen stated, “we're very mindful of how much price has been pushed through the system...do we believe we'll get price? Yes...we're trying to take a measured approach there as we think about not only the customer dynamics, but what does it mean from a margin perspective.”
  • Kristen Owen, Oppenheimer, inquired about used inventory movement and pricing. Rohleder said, “the focus here is really around high horsepower tractors in North America. And what we've seen in that segment is some stabilization...pace...is still a bit unknown, and there's a lot of factors in play.”
  • Stephen Volkmann, Jefferies, asked about the ultimate goal regarding tariffs. Jepsen responded, “ultimately, the goal would be to find ways to protect margin, and that's not just price. That's what we're doing from a cost structure perspective how we can add value.”

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts displayed a neutral to slightly skeptical tone, with multiple questions pressing for clarity on margin implications, tariff sharing, and future pricing strategies.
  • Management maintained a measured and confident stance in prepared remarks but showed more caution and detail in Q&A, especially on tariffs and margin pressures, using phrases like “measured approach” and “we're very mindful.”
  • Compared to last quarter, management's tone shifted from quiet confidence to more explicit caution and adaptability, citing increased volatility and the need to broaden guidance.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • The current quarter saw the broadening of net income guidance, whereas last quarter’s outlook range was narrower.
  • Tariff impacts and mitigation dominated this quarter's discussion, while currency headwinds were emphasized in the previous quarter.
  • Management now places more focus on technology adoption and SaaS expansion, highlighting growing orders and renewals, especially in Brazil and North America.
  • Analysts’ questions shifted from inventory management and production cadence last quarter to tariff mitigation, margin protection, and pricing strategy this quarter.
  • Management's confidence level in operational execution remains, but guidance language has grown more cautious, reflecting heightened uncertainty.

Risks and Concerns

  • The company cited significant uncertainty in global trade policy and tariff impacts, with an anticipated $500 million pre-tax effect in fiscal 2025.
  • Management identified ongoing risks from elevated used inventory levels, high interest rates, and subdued demand in certain segments, particularly small Ag and turf.
  • Mitigation strategies include certification for trade exemptions, optimizing sourcing, and continued cost discipline.
  • Management acknowledged the difficulty in passing on tariffs to customers mid-year due to full order books, indicating future pricing may reflect these costs.

Final Takeaway

Deere & Company navigated a volatile quarter marked by tariff headwinds and shifting market dynamics by delivering strong operational margins, expanding its smart industrial and technology initiatives, and maintaining disciplined cost controls. While global uncertainty has led to a widened net income outlook, management remains committed to customer focus, U.S. manufacturing investment, and the continued rollout of precision agriculture solutions. Proactive mitigation strategies and measured pricing actions are central to navigating the remainder of fiscal 2025, with a clear emphasis on protecting margins and supporting both customers and shareholders amid an evolving global environment.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: Crescent Capital BDC signals stable dividend and portfolio resilience while navigating non-accrual uptick
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Crescent Capital BDC, Inc. (CCAP) Q1 2025

Management View

  • CEO Jason Breaux reported net investment income of $16.6 million or $0.45 per share for the first quarter, compared to $20.5 million or $0.55 per share in the fourth quarter. Breaux attributed the NII decline to "the impact of lower base rates resulting from the two FOMC rate cuts during the fourth quarter of last year, the roll off of certain one time and non-recurring items and a reduction in dividend income from the Logan JV resulting from the end of the reinvestment period." He noted an increase in loans on non-accrual, which reached 3.5% of debt investments at cost and 1.8% at fair value.
  • Looking ahead, Breaux stated, "We believe that this quarter's earnings are reflective of our earnings baseline in the near term. We have potential near term tailwinds from our SPV asset based facility repricing and rightsizing that we completed at the beginning of the quarter." He emphasized the company’s focus on first lien loans, diversified across 191 companies, and stated, "Our private credit origination platform activity has provided us the opportunity to nearly double the number of portfolio companies in our portfolio since listing, even after taking into account prior acquisitions."
  • Breaux highlighted a second quarter 2025 regular dividend of $0.42 per share and a $0.05 per share special dividend related to undistributed taxable income. He commented, "We have earned our dividends since CCAP's inception and we note that our NII continues to be in excess of our base dividend in the first quarter."
  • President Henry Chung explained, "Gross deployment in the first quarter totaled $105 million...of which 98% was in first lien investments. During the quarter, we closed 10 new platform investments totaling $78 million."
  • CFO Gerhard Lombard stated, "Our net investment income per share of $0.45 for the first quarter of 2025 compared to $0.55 per share for the prior quarter." He cited four drivers for the NII change: lower base rates, runoff of one-time non-recurring income, dividend income decline from the Logan JV, and loans placed on non-accrual during the quarter. Lombard reported stockholders' equity of $727 million and net asset value per share of $19.62.

Outlook

  • Breaux noted, "Looking ahead, we believe that this quarter's earnings are reflective of our earnings baseline in the near term." He identified "potential near term tailwinds from our SPV asset based facility repricing and rightsizing that we completed at the beginning of the quarter, as well as the full quarter impact of our portfolio at target leverage."
  • The outlook does not reflect the impact of further loans placed on non-accrual or changes in base rates.
  • The company declared a regular Q2 2025 dividend of $0.42 per share and a special $0.05 dividend for June.

Financial Results

  • Net investment income for Q1 2025 was $16.6 million ($0.45 per share), down from $20.5 million ($0.55 per share) in Q4 2024.
  • GAAP earnings per share for Q1 2025 were $0.11, with net investment income offset by $0.34 per share of net unrealized and realized losses.
  • Investments at fair value totaled just over $1.6 billion across 191 companies.
  • The weighted average yield of income producing securities at cost declined 50 basis points quarter over quarter to 10.4%.
  • Net deployment for the quarter was approximately $27 million.
  • Debt to equity ratio increased from 1.19x to 1.25x.

Q&A

  • Paul Johnson, KBW: Asked about the non-accrual status and restructuring for New Era Technology. Henry Chung responded that the mark reflects expectations for restructuring, while Breaux clarified, "New Era on our books has not yet restructured." Johnson inquired about Crescent's approach to workouts; Breaux stated, "Crescent's approach to difficult situations, typically restructurings, is really first and foremost to preserve our capital...we will try to be constructive and find ways to come to an arrangement...If that is not an available option...we can go in a different direction."
  • Johnson also asked about the proportion of Crescent-led or originated deals. Breaux said, "About 8% of the total fair value is acquired assets. The remaining 92% are loans that we acquired...the vast majority is, Crescent originated assets."
  • Robert Dodd, Raymond James: Inquired about the big sequential dividend drop in the Logan JV. Chung explained, "There's actually about a month and a half timing mismatch between our quarter end and when those payment determination dates are...what you'll see is the most kind of lumpy part of the cash distributions, which are related to the equity tranche."
  • Dodd asked about visibility into new non-accruals. Breaux replied, "They were all prior watch list names...for each of these names, there were developments at the respective borrowers...that warranted that redetermination."
  • Finian O'Shea, Wells Fargo: Probed for sector concentration in non-accruals, particularly software. Chung clarified only two were software, both with company-specific drivers.
  • Mickey Schleien, Ladenburg: Asked about market sentiment and spread outlook. Breaux stated, "In terms of outlook and deal activity, we started the year with pretty good activity...that was certainly impacted, by April 9, and the 90 day pause." On buybacks, Breaux added, "This is something that we continue to evaluate, especially now where our shares are trading relative to where they were trading at the beginning of the year."

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts expressed concern about the rise in non-accruals, the volatility of Logan JV income, and the potential for further credit deterioration, with a slightly negative tone in repeated questions about portfolio risk and sector headwinds.
  • Management maintained a neutral to slightly defensive tone, emphasizing long-term discipline and capital preservation, as seen in statements like "our foremost interest and priority...being to protect our investment" and clarifying that "these are companies that we've kind of noted as watch list companies, some for several quarters now."
  • Compared to the previous quarter, analysts’ tone shifted from focusing on portfolio diversification and sector risk to a more pressing scrutiny of non-accruals and income volatility. Management tone remained disciplined, but with more emphasis on risk mitigation and explanations for underperformance drivers than before.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • Net investment income per share declined from $0.55 in Q4 2024 to $0.45 in Q1 2025, with management attributing the change to base rate cuts and non-recurring income roll-off.
  • Non-accruals increased to 3.5% at cost (from 2.2% in Q4) and 1.8% at fair value (from 0.9%), with new non-accruals being former watch list names rather than sudden surprises.
  • The weighted average yield on income-producing securities dropped 50 basis points, reflecting the impact of base rate changes.
  • Dividend policy remained consistent, with the regular dividend unchanged and a special dividend continuing as planned.
  • Management’s focus shifted from projecting optimism about increased M&A and deal flow (Q4) to emphasizing risk discipline and near-term earnings stability (Q1).
  • Analysts’ questions moved from portfolio construction and risk management to more pointed concerns regarding non-accruals and income volatility.

Risks and Concerns

  • The company cited an increase in loans on non-accrual and the potential for further placements as a key risk.
  • Management acknowledged the impact of base rate cuts on income, as well as lumpy dividend income from the Logan JV due to CLO deleveraging.
  • Exposure to tariffs was reviewed, with management stating, "the overall direct material exposure is modest at 4%" and highlighting mitigating factors like price pass-through ability.
  • Analyst concerns centered on the visibility and sector concentration of non-accruals, as well as the sustainability of portfolio income.

Final Takeaway

Crescent Capital BDC’s first quarter of 2025 was marked by a decrease in net investment income, a rise in non-accrual loans, and continued discipline in portfolio construction and dividend policy. Management emphasized that the current earnings represent a near-term baseline, supported by a diversified, predominantly first lien portfolio and proactive risk management. The outlook remains cautious, with close attention to macroeconomic factors and portfolio credit quality, while maintaining confidence in the company’s long-term dividend coverage and stability.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: Vizsla Silver surges on cash and stock deal for Mexico claim package
data.description:
Vizsla Silver (NYSE:VZLA) +7.1% in Thursday's trading after saying it agreed to acquire the Santa Fe mining claims package in western Mexico, including both production and exploration concessions, to a group including Eduardo de la Peña Gaitán for $1.43M in cash and 2.75M common shares.

The miner said it acquired the 12,229-hectare Santa Fe project located to the south of its flagship Panuco project, which benefits from permitted on-site production infrastructure including an operating 350 metric tons/day mill situated along the highly prospective Panuco-San Dimas corridor.

"With an option agreement now in place on the Santa Fe production concessions, Vizsla Silver has the potential to bolster its overall production profile well beyond the 20.2M silver equivalent oz of initial annual production envisioned for Panuco Project #1," President and CEO Michael Konnert said.

Konnert also said mine production and historic drilling completed to date covers less than 12% of the overall Santa Fe property package, and with previous geophysical surveys highlighting at least four key target areas outboard of the underground mine, the Santa Fe concessions provide increased exploration upside along trend of known mining centers.
